quiz.title: Unit 7 Quiz 3, Grade 11 Chemistry
quiz.subtitle: Mole-Mass Conversions and Avogadro's Number
quiz.subject: Chemistry
quiz.topic: Mole Concept, Molar Mass Calculations, Converting Moles to Mass, Converting Mass to Moles, Avogadro's Number, Particles to Moles Conversions

questions.question: How many moles are in 36.04 g of water (H₂O)?
questions.explanation: Molar mass of H₂O = 18.02 g/mol. Moles = mass / molar mass = 36.04 g / 18.02 g/mol = 2.00 mol.

questions.question: What mass of sodium chloride (NaCl) contains 0.500 mol?
questions.explanation: Molar mass of NaCl = 58.44 g/mol. Mass = moles × molar mass = 0.500 mol × 58.44 g/mol = 29.22 g.

questions.question: How many moles are present in 88.02 g of carbon dioxide (CO₂)?
questions.explanation: Molar mass of CO₂ = 44.01 g/mol. Moles = mass / molar mass = 88.02 g / 44.01 g/mol = 2.00 mol.

questions.question: The mass of 3.50 mol of glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆) is:
questions.explanation: Molar mass of glucose = 180.16 g/mol. Mass = moles × molar mass = 3.50 mol × 180.16 g/mol = 630.56 g ≈ 630.6 g.

questions.question: How many moles are in 9.01 g of ammonia (NH₃)?
questions.explanation: Molar mass of NH₃ = 17.03 g/mol. Moles = mass / molar mass = 9.01 g / 17.03 g/mol = 0.529 mol.

questions.question: What is the mass of 0.250 mol of calcium carbonate (CaCO₃)?
questions.explanation: Molar mass of CaCO₃ = 100.09 g/mol. Mass = moles × molar mass = 0.250 mol × 100.09 g/mol = 25.02 g.

questions.question: How many moles are contained in 196.2 g of sulfuric acid (H₂SO₄)?
questions.explanation: Molar mass of H₂SO₄ = 98.08 g/mol. Moles = mass / molar mass = 196.2 g / 98.08 g/mol = 2.00 mol.

questions.question: 1 mole of any gas at STP occupies _____ L.
questions.explanation: At STP (Standard Temperature and Pressure: 0°C and 1 atm), one mole of any ideal gas occupies 22.4 liters. This is known as the molar volume of a gas at STP.

questions.question: What is the number of hydrogen atoms in 1 mole of CH₄?
questions.explanation: One mole of CH₄ contains 4 moles of hydrogen atoms. Number of H atoms = 4 mol × $6.022 \times 10^{23}$ atoms/mol = $2.4088 \times 10^{24}$ hydrogen atoms.

questions.question: One mole of any substance contains how many particles?
questions.explanation: Avogadro's number is $6.022 \times 10^{23}$ particles per mole. This constant represents the number of atoms, molecules, or formula units in one mole of any substance.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is used to convert between _____ and number of particles.
questions.explanation: Avogadro's number ($6.022 \times 10^{23}$ particles/mol) is used to convert between moles and the number of particles (atoms, molecules, ions, etc.).

questions.question: How many molecules are in 2 moles of CO₂?
questions.explanation: Number of molecules = moles × Avogadro's number = 2 mol × $6.022 \times 10^{23}$ molecules/mol = $1.2044 \times 10^{24}$ molecules.

questions.question: The number of atoms in 3 moles of He is:
questions.explanation: Helium is monatomic, so each mole contains $6.022 \times 10^{23}$ atoms. Number of atoms = 3 mol × $6.022 \times 10^{23}$ atoms/mol = $1.8066 \times 10^{24}$ atoms.

questions.question: How many atoms are in 0.5 mole of Al?
questions.explanation: Aluminum is monatomic. Number of atoms = moles × Avogadro's number = 0.5 mol × $6.022 \times 10^{23}$ atoms/mol = $3.011 \times 10^{23}$ atoms.

questions.question: How many oxygen atoms are in 4 moles of CO₂?
questions.explanation: Each CO₂ molecule contains 2 oxygen atoms. First, find molecules of CO₂ = 4 mol × $6.022 \times 10^{23}$ = $2.4088 \times 10^{24}$ molecules. Then, oxygen atoms = molecules × 2 = $2.4088 \times 10^{24} × 2 = 4.8176 \times 10^{24}$ oxygen atoms.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered mole-mass conversions and Avogadro's number! ⚛️🧮
scoreMessages.70: Good job! Review the relationship between moles, mass, and number of particles.
scoreMessages.50: Satisfactory. Focus on using the correct formula for mole conversions.
scoreMessages.0: Please review the mole concept, molar mass calculations, and Avogadro's number.
